1: tō angelō tēs en Ephesō ekklēsias grapson: 'To the messenger of the church in Ephesus write.' Each letter is addressed to the angelos (messenger/angel) of the church — this figure is either the church's guardian angel or, more likely given the context of human accountability, the leader/bishop who embodies and represents the community. Kratōn tous hepta asteras (holding the seven stars): kratōn is present participle — ongoing, continuous possession and governance. Peripatōn en mesō: walking among (present participle) — active, continuous pastoral presence.
2: oida ta erga sou kai ton kopon kai tēn hypomonēn sou: 'I know your deeds, your labor, and your patient endurance.' Oida (perfect, used as present): I have come to know and know now — Christ's knowledge is comprehensive and current. Kopos (labor) is more intense than general ergon (work): it implies toiling effort, wearying exertion. Ou dynē bastasai kakous: you are not able to tolerate evil people — bastasai (bear, carry) suggests they have tested false teachers and found them wanting, bearing the weight of that discernment.
3: kai hypomonēn echeis kai ebastasas dia to onoma mou kai ou kekopiakas: 'You have endurance and you have borne burdens for my name and have not grown weary.' The aorist ebastasas (you have borne/carried) describes completed past endurance; ou kekopiakas (you have not grown weary) is perfect — the state of not-having-wearied persists. The Ephesian church's endurance record is strong; the contrast with v. 4 is deliberate.
4: alla echō kata sou hoti tēn agapēn sou tēn prōtēn aphēkes: 'But I have this against you: you have abandoned your first love.' Aphēkes (aorist): you let go, you released, you abandoned — a deliberate action, not gradual drift. Tēn agapēn sou tēn prōtēn: the first/original love (prōtēn = first in time, prior love). The Ephesians' doctrinal vigilance (vv. 2-3) has come at the cost of the relational vitality that animated their original faith.
5: mnēmoneue pothen peptōkas kai metanoēson kai ta prōta erga poiēson: 'Remember from where you have fallen; repent; and do the first works.' Three aorist imperatives in sequence — remember, repent, do — forming a program of restoration. Peptōkas (perfect): you have fallen and remain in the fallen state. Kinēsō tēn lychnian sou ek tou topou autēs: I will move/remove your lampstand from its place — removal is the consequence of non-repentance; the church ceases to function as a light-bearer.
6: alla touto echeis hoti miseis ta erga tōn Nikolaitōn ha kagō misō: 'But you have this: you hate the practices of the Nicolaitans, which I also hate.' Miseis and misō (present tense) — strong active hatred, the same verb applied to both the church and to Christ; the community's revulsion at the Nicolaitan practice is validated by Christ's own attitude. The Nicolaitans are associated with the Balaam-teaching (v. 14-15): eating food sacrificed to idols and sexual immorality, possibly an antinomian group that accommodated to Greco-Roman social norms.
7: tō nikōnti dōsō autō phagein ek tou xylou tēs zōēs: 'To the one who overcomes I will give to eat from the tree of life.' Nikōnti (present participle, dative): to the one who is conquering — ongoing victory, not a single past act. The closing formula ho echōn ous akousatō ti to pneuma legei tais ekklēsiais appears at the end of each letter: the Spirit's address to one church is for all seven churches — the letter is simultaneously specific and universal.
8: ho prōtos kai ho eschatos, hos egeneto nekros kai ezēsen: 'the First and the Last, who died and came to life.' The YHWH-title from Isa 44:6 (first and last) is linked to the resurrection fact: the one who claims the divine identity-marker is the one who died and rose. Egeneto nekros kai ezēsen: became dead and lived — both aorists, two completed historical events. To the Smyrna church facing poverty and potential death, Christ presents his own death-and-life as the paradigm for what they face.
9: oida sou tēn thlipsin kai tēn ptōcheian, alla plousios ei: 'I know your affliction and your poverty — yet you are rich.' The contrast is direct: material poverty (ptōcheia, utter destitution) against spiritual wealth. Blasphēmian tōn legontōn Ioudaious einai heautous kai ouk eisin alla synagōgē tou satana: the slander from those claiming to be Jews but not being so. The phrase synagōgē tou satana (synagogue of Satan) is polemical: those who claim the covenant name but oppose the Messiah have become, functionally, the assembly of the adversary rather than the assembly of God.
10: mēden phobou ha melleis paschein: 'Fear nothing of what you are about to suffer.' Melleis paschein (about to suffer): imminent, foreseeable suffering. Deka hēmeras (ten days): a limited, defined period — alluding to the ten-day testing of Daniel and his companions (Dan 1:12-15); the number signals that the trial, however severe, is bounded by God's will. Stephanos tēs zōēs (crown of life): stephanos is the victor's laurel wreath, not the royal diadem (diadēma) — the image is athletic/competitive: those who compete faithfully receive the life-wreath.
11: ho nikōn ou mē adikēthē ek tou deuterou thanatou: 'The one who overcomes will not be harmed by the second death.' The double negative ou mē with subjunctive is the strongest Greek negation — absolute certainty of exemption. Ho deuteros thanatos (the second death) is Revelation's term for permanent separation from God after final judgment (20:6, 14; 21:8) — the lake of fire. The promise to faithful Smyrna: physical death cannot touch them where it ultimately matters.
12: ho echōn tēn rhomphian tēn distomon tēn oxeian: 'the one who has the sharp double-edged sword.' The self-identification of Christ to Pergamum uses the sword-from-mouth imagery of 1:16 — relevant because Pergamum was the capital of Roman Asia and the seat of Roman judicial authority; Christ presents himself as the ultimate judge whose word supersedes Rome's verdict. The Roman proconsul exercised ius gladii (right of the sword) — the power of execution. Christ's sword-of-the-mouth counters this claim.
13: ho thronos tou satana: 'the throne of Satan' — Pergamum was the location of the imperial cult in Asia, with a major altar to Augustus/Rome, and the great altar of Zeus (possibly the altar now reconstructed in Berlin). Antipas ho martys mou ho pistos: Antipas my faithful witness — the use of martys (witness) for one who died for the faith marks the semantic evolution toward 'martyr.' Ho katechoumenoi tēn didachēn Balaam: those holding to the teaching of Balaam — a syncretistic accommodation to imperial cult practices.
14: tēn didachēn Balaam, hos edidasken ton Balak balein skandalon enōpion tōn huiōn Israēl, phagein eidōlothyta kai porneusai: 'the teaching of Balaam, who taught Balak to put a stumbling block before the children of Israel, so that they might eat food sacrificed to idols and practice sexual immorality.' Skandalon (stumbling block/snare): the metaphor of a trap. Eidōlothyta (idol-food): food offered in sacrifice to Greco-Roman deities — eating it at temple banquets was socially expected in trade guilds; the refusal to eat separated Christians from normal social life. Porneusai: either literal sexual immorality at temple banquets or, metaphorically, spiritual unfaithfulness (syncretism as adultery against God).
15: homoiōs echeis kai sy tous kratountas tēn didachēn tōn Nikolaitōn: 'Likewise you also have some who hold the teaching of the Nicolaitans.' Homoiōs (likewise): the Nicolaitan teaching parallels the Balaam-teaching — they are variants of the same accommodation. The church at Pergamum tolerates these teachers; this is the opposite problem from Ephesus, who rightly detected and rejected false apostles (2:2) but lost their first love.
16: metanoēson oun; ei de mē, erchomai soi tachy kai polemēsō met autōn en tē rhomphaiai tou stomatos mou: 'Repent therefore; if not, I will come to you quickly and make war against them with the sword of my mouth.' The judgment falls on the false teachers (met autōn, against them), not the whole church — but the church's tolerance of them means Christ's coming-to-judge will affect the community. Erchomai tachy: coming quickly (the same phrase used of the parousia in 3:11; 22:7, 12, 20 — Christ's judicial coming and his final coming use the same language).
17: tō nikōnti dōsō autō tou manna tou kekrymmenom kai dōsō autō psēphon leukēn kai epi tēn psēphon onoma kainon gegrammenon: 'To the one who overcomes I will give some of the hidden manna, and a white stone with a new name written on it.' Manna kekrymmenon (hidden manna): Jewish tradition held that Jeremiah hid the golden jar of manna before the exile (2 Macc 2:4-8) for eschatological restoration; the promise reverses the idol-food temptation with true heavenly bread. Psēphos leukē (white stone): possibly an acquittal token (white = innocent in Greek judicial practice), or an invitation tessera for a banquet (social entry token). Onoma kainon: a new name known only to the recipient — personal, intimate identity restored by Christ.
18: ho huios tou theou, ho echōn tous ophthalmous autou hōs phloga pyros kai hoi podes autou homoioi chalkolibanō: 'the Son of God, whose eyes are like blazing fire and whose feet are like burnished bronze.' Ho huios tou theou is the only occurrence of this exact title in Revelation — addressing Thyatira, where the imperial cult identified the emperor's son with divine sonship. Christ's sonship displaces the emperor's claim. The fiery eyes (Dan 10:6) signal penetrating omniscience; the bronze feet signal unbreakable authority (the elements from the throne-room vision applied in a context of imminent judgment).
19: oida sou ta erga kai tēn agapēn kai tēn pistin kai tēn diakonian kai tēn hypomonēn sou: 'I know your deeds — your love, faith, service, and endurance.' Four virtues in a single catalogue: agapē (love), pistis (faith/faithfulness), diakonia (service/ministry), hypomonē (patient endurance). Thyatira's profile is the strongest of the churches in active virtue. Kai ta eschata pleiōna tōn prōtōn: your recent deeds surpass your earlier ones — the community is growing, not declining.
20: Ieza bel hē legousa heautēn prophētin: 'Jezebel, who calls herself a prophetess.' The name Jezebel (Ieza bel) is a pseudonym, alluding to the Phoenician queen of 1 Kgs 16-21 — the historical Jezebel led Ahab into Baal worship and killed the prophets of YHWH. The church at Thyatira tolerates a woman leader who falsely presents herself as a prophetess and promotes exactly the Balaam-pattern of idol-food and sexual immorality. Didaskalei kai plana tous emous doulous: she teaches and misleads my servants — planaō (mislead/deceive) is the word for the wandering of sheep and the deception of false teachers.
21: kai edōka autē chronon hina metanoēsē, kai ou thelei metanoēsai ek tēs porneias autēs: 'I gave her time to repent, and she does not want to repent of her sexual immorality.' Edōka chronon (I gave her time): the aorist marks a specific prior opportunity that was not taken. Ou thelei (she does not want/will not): not inability but refusal — the fault is volitional. The seriousness of the judgment that follows (vv. 22-23) is grounded in this refusal after extended opportunity.
22: ballō autēn eis klinēn kai tous moicheuntas met autēs eis thlipsin megalēn: 'I will throw her onto a bed of suffering, and those who commit adultery with her into great tribulation.' Klinē (bed): the judgment mirrors the sin — the bed of immorality becomes the bed of illness and suffering. Moicheuntas: committing adultery; both literal (sexual immorality at temple events) and metaphorical (spiritual adultery against God through idolatry). Ean mē metanoēsōsin: unless they repent (aorist subjunctive) — the conditional shows the judgment is not final until the possibility of repentance passes.
23: ego eimi ho eraunōn nephrous kai kardias: 'I am the one who searches kidneys and hearts.' Nephrous (kidneys): in Hebrew anthropology, the kidneys are the seat of deep emotion, conscience, and hidden motivation (Jer 17:10; Ps 7:9). The compound nephrous kai kardias (kidneys and hearts) is drawn from the OT divine-judge idiom — the one who searches the hidden depths of human motivation. This is a claim to omniscience about inner dispositions, not just outward behavior.
24: ta bathē tou satana: 'the deep things of Satan.' This is evidently the self-description of the false teacher's followers — they boasted of knowing the 'deep things' (possibly a gnosticizing claim to spiritual depth through transgression). The MKT and most modern translations read this as the false teachers' own language, used ironically by Christ. Ou ballō eph hymas allo baros: I will not put any other burden on you — the church members who remain faithful are not required to bear more than holding fast.
25: plēn ho echete kratēsate achri hou an hēxō: 'Only hold fast to what you have until I come.' Kratēsate (aorist imperative): seize and hold — a decisive act of grasping. Achri hou an hēxō: until I come — the parousia as the horizon that defines faithful endurance; one holds not forever but until the one who is coming arrives.
26: kai ho nikōn kai ho tērōn achri telous ta erga mou, dōsō autō exousian epi tōn ethnōn: 'To the one who overcomes and keeps my works to the end, I will give authority over the nations.' Two present participles (nikōn and tērōn) — overcoming and keeping are ongoing activities, not past accomplishments. Achri telous (to the end): perseverance through the full duration. Exousian epi tōn ethnōn: authority over the nations — sharing in Christ's messianic rule (Ps 2:8-9).
27: kai poimanei autous en rhabdō sidēra hōs ta skeuē ta keramika syntribetai: 'He will shepherd them with an iron rod and shatter them like clay vessels.' Poimanei (will shepherd/rule): from poimainō, the shepherd-verb — the iron-rod rule is described with the same word as pastoral care. The LXX of Ps 2:9 uses poimainō for 'rule/break,' preserving the ambiguity of the Hebrew raah. Kathōs kagō eilēpha para tou patros mou: just as I myself have received from my Father — the authority is derived, not autonomous.
28: dōsō autō ton astera ton prōinon: 'I will give him the morning star.' The morning star (astēr prōinos) is identified as Christ himself in 22:16 ('I am the root and descendant of David, the bright morning star'). The promise to give the morning star is thus a promise to give Christ himself — the fullest possible eschatological gift. The background may be Num 24:17 ('a star shall come out of Jacob'), the messianic star-prophecy that was applied to the coming ruler in multiple Jewish contexts.
29: ho echōn ous akousatō ti to pneuma legei tais ekklēsiais: 'Let anyone who has an ear hear what the Spirit says to the churches.' The closing formula appears in all seven letters. Its movement from singular (addressed to one church) to plural (tais ekklēsiais, the churches) signals that each letter addresses all seven — the community that hears one letter is called to hear all. The Spirit is the one speaking through the glorified Christ: Christology and pneumatology are inseparable in Revelation's vision-structure.
